title: California Coast & Parks
subtitle: Pasadena → Big Sur → San Francisco → Yosemite → Sequoia · 7 days / 6 nights

days.title: Pasadena → Santa Barbara → Solvang → SLO / Pismo

items.title: Departure from Pasadena
items.notes: Start and end point of the loop. Full tank, coffee in hand, and off you go on US-101.

items.title: Leave by 7:00 AM
items.notes: A day that packs in Santa Barbara + Santa Ynez + SLO: doable, but no lingering at minor stops. LA morning traffic: the earlier you leave, the better.

items.title: Pasadena → Santa Barbara
items.notes: US-101 N. About 100 miles.

items.title: Stearns Wharf + waterfront
items.notes: A compact visit: pier, beach and palm trees. Park on the wharf or along Cabrillo Blvd.

items.title: Old Mission Santa Barbara (optional)
items.notes: Only if it appeals to you: the 'Queen of the Missions'. Otherwise, spend more time on State Street.

items.title: State Street — coffee / quick lunch
items.notes: The main pedestrian street, plenty of quick options.

items.title: Santa Barbara → Solvang
items.notes: Via CA-154 (San Marcos Pass, scenic) or US-101.

items.title: Solvang / Santa Ynez Valley
items.notes: Danish village: charming but very touristy. A scenic break of 1–1.5 hours max. Try an aebleskiver!

items.title: Downtown San Luis Obispo
items.notes: Stroll through downtown: Higuera Street, Bubblegum Alley, Mission Plaza.

items.title: Sunset at Pismo Pier
items.notes: Ocean sunset from the pier. Alternatively, Avila Beach — smaller and more intimate.

items.title: Dinner in Pismo / SLO
items.notes: Pismo = clam chowder (Splash Café). SLO = wider choice of restaurants downtown.

items.title: Night 1 — SLO or Pismo/Avila Beach
items.notes: SLO for convenience and restaurants; Pismo/Avila for coastal atmosphere.

days.title: SLO → Morro Bay → Hearst Castle → Big Sur → Carmel

items.title: Check Highway 1 on Caltrans
items.notes: The trickiest day of the trip. Check the actual road status a few days ahead AND the morning of. Plan B if closed: Paso Robles → US-101 → Salinas → Monterey.

items.title: Morro Rock + breakfast
items.notes: Short stop: views of the 'Gibraltar of the Pacific', coffee by the little harbor. Keep an eye out for sea otters!

items.title: Morro Bay → San Simeon (Elephant Seal Vista Point)
Elephant Seal Vista Point
items.notes: A quick but near-mandatory stop: hundreds of elephant seals on the beach. Free, with a scenic boardwalk.

items.title: Hearst Castle — only if it's a priority
items.notes: DECISION TIME: if you really care about it, book the FIRST tour of the morning. Otherwise skip it and give Big Sur more time (recommended with 7 days). First candidate for the cut.

items.title: San Simeon → Ragged Point
items.notes: The real Highway 1 begins: curves and cliffs.

items.title: Ragged Point — views + quick lunch
items.notes: The 'Gateway to Big Sur': panoramic terrace and snack bar.

items.title: Ragged Point → McWay Falls
items.notes: The most spectacular stretch of the coast: drive unhurried, with short stops at the turnouts.

items.title: McWay Falls — Julia Pfeiffer Burns SP
items.notes: The waterfall that drops straight onto the beach. The Overlook Trail is very short (half a mile). Parking $10, or roadside spots.

items.title: McWay Falls → Pfeiffer Big Sur SP
Pfeiffer Big Sur State Park — short stop
items.notes: Coast redwoods along the Big Sur River. A brief stop, just long enough to breathe in the forest.

items.title: Bixby Creek Bridge
items.notes: The most photographed bridge in California. Turnout on the north side (Castle Rock viewpoint). Best light in the afternoon.

items.title: Stroll through Carmel-by-the-Sea
items.notes: Fairy-tale cottages, art galleries, and Ocean Avenue all the way down to the beach.

items.title: Dinner in Carmel or Monterey
items.notes: Carmel = more atmosphere. Monterey = more choice and better prices.

items.title: Night 2 — Monterey or Carmel
items.notes: Carmel for the atmosphere, Monterey for choice/prices. Book well in advance!

days.title: Point Lobos → 17-Mile Drive → Santa Cruz → San Francisco

items.title: Point Lobos: arrive EARLY
items.notes: Parking is very limited (~150 cars): ideally arrive before 9:30. This day sacrifices the Monterey Bay Aquarium — fitting it in means cutting Santa Cruz / Half Moon Bay (not recommended).

links.label: Monterey Bay Aquarium (if you change your mind)

items.title: Point Lobos State Natural Reserve
items.notes: One of the most beautiful stops of the whole trip. Do: Sea Lion Point Trail, Cypress Grove Trail, Bird Island Trail (time permitting). Entry $10/car.

items.title: Carmel Beach
items.notes: White-sand beach framed by cypress trees; a quick stop.

items.title: 17-Mile Drive — short version
items.notes: Lone Cypress + Pebble Beach viewpoint. Toll ~$12/car (refunded if you have lunch at the restaurants along the route).

items.title: Pebble Beach → Santa Cruz
items.notes: CA-1 N along the bay. Quick lunch on the road or in Santa Cruz.

items.title: Santa Cruz Beach Boardwalk
items.notes: Historic beachfront amusement park: even just 45 minutes for the atmosphere. Ride the Giant Dipper if you're up for it!

items.title: Natural Bridges State Beach
items.notes: Rock arch rising from the sea; a quick stop.

items.title: Pigeon Point Lighthouse (if on schedule)
items.notes: One of the tallest lighthouses on the West Coast. Only if you're not running late.

items.title: Half Moon Bay (if there's still daylight)
items.notes: One last breath of ocean air before the city.

items.title: Half Moon Bay → San Francisco
items.notes: CA-1 N through Pacifica, entering the city from the south.

items.title: Dinner in San Francisco
items.notes: First evening in the city: North Beach (Italian) or the Mission (Mexican) are both great areas.

items.title: Night 3 — San Francisco (1 of 2)
items.notes: Same hotel for 2 nights: pick a convenient area (Union Square, Fisherman's Wharf or the Marina). Watch out for hotel parking fees (~$40-70/night).

days.title: San Francisco + Golden Gate + Marin Headlands

items.title: Just one full day in SF
items.notes: Not much, but consistent with a coast + parks trip. Do NOT add Napa, Muir Woods or Point Reyes: they would throw the whole trip off balance. City in the morning, Golden Gate/Marin in the afternoon.

items.title: Breakfast at the Ferry Building
items.notes: Gourmet marketplace on the Embarcadero. On Saturday mornings there's a farmers market too.

items.title: Embarcadero → North Beach on foot
items.notes: Along the waterfront, then into the Italian quarter: Washington Square, City Lights Bookstore.

items.title: Chinatown
items.notes: The oldest in North America: Grant Avenue + Waverly Place, fortune cookie factory in Ross Alley.

items.title: Cable Car + Nob Hill
items.notes: Hop on the Powell-Hyde line: views of Alcatraz and Lombard Street. $8 per ride (or a MuniMobile day pass).

items.title: Quick lunch in North Beach / Chinatown
Painted Ladies (if you're interested)
items.notes: The Victorian houses of Alamo Square. Skippable if you're running late.

items.title: Golden Gate Bridge — Welcome Center + walk
items.notes: Walk out onto the bridge on the east side (pedestrians). Dizzying and beautiful.

items.title: Battery Spencer
items.notes: THE iconic view of the bridge from above, on the Marin side. Tiny parking lot: be patient for a spot.

items.title: Marin Headlands — Hawk Hill + Rodeo Beach
items.notes: Scenic Conzelman Road, then down to Rodeo Beach (dark sand).

items.title: Sausalito (only if you have energy left)
items.notes: Drinks with a skyline view. Alternatively: head back and rest up before Yosemite.

items.title: Dinner in San Francisco
items.notes: Last evening in the city. Tomorrow is an EARLY start.

items.title: Yosemite 2026: no timed-entry reservation, but…
items.notes: No timed-entry reservation is needed, but you do have to pay the entrance fee ($35/car, valid 7 days) or carry an America the Beautiful pass. The real issue: parking and traffic → arrive early. Fill up on gas before entering.

items.title: San Francisco → Groveland
items.notes: I-580 → I-205 → CA-120 E via Oakdale (the 'Gateway to Yosemite': last real coffee stop).

items.title: Groveland → Big Oak Flat Entrance → Yosemite Valley
items.notes: CA-120, northwest entrance. Slow, scenic driving inside the park.

items.title: Tunnel View
items.notes: THE Yosemite panorama: El Capitan, Half Dome and Bridalveil all in one frame. Mandatory first stop.

items.title: Bridalveil Fall
items.notes: Short, easy trail to the base of the waterfall (expect spray!).

items.title: Lunch in the Valley
items.notes: Picnic, or the Village Store / Base Camp Eatery in Yosemite Village.

items.title: El Capitan Meadow
items.notes: Look straight up: with a bit of luck you'll spot climbers on the wall (binoculars help).

items.title: Lower Yosemite Fall
items.notes: Easy 1-mile loop to the base of North America's tallest waterfall.

items.title: Cook's Meadow at sunset
items.notes: Golden light on Half Dome and Yosemite Falls. The perfect way to end the day.

items.title: Dinner in the Valley or in El Portal
items.notes: Limited options: best not to eat too late.

items.title: Night 5 — THE MOST IMPORTANT one to book
items.notes: Order of preference: 1) inside Yosemite Valley (if you can find a spot), 2) El Portal (best compromise), 3) Mariposa (farther but affordable), 4) Oakhurst only if you're heading out south afterwards. Sleep too far away and the itinerary loses its magic.

items.title: Check that Glacier Point Road is open
items.notes: OPTION A (recommended): Glacier Point + Washburn Point if the road is open (closed in winter/spring). OPTION B: Mariposa Grove instead of Glacier Point — though it overlaps a bit with day 7 at Sequoia.

items.title: Valley → Glacier Point
items.notes: Wawona Road + Glacier Point Road. Leave very early.

items.title: Glacier Point
items.notes: The grand overview of all of Yosemite: Half Dome face to face, Vernal and Nevada Falls from above. Worth every mile of the detour.

items.title: Washburn Point
items.notes: Half a mile from Glacier Point, a different angle on Half Dome. Quick stop.

items.title: Sentinel Dome or Taft Point (short hike, optional)
items.notes: ~2.2 miles round trip each. Sentinel Dome = 360° views. Taft Point = a heart-stopping sheer drop. Only if your legs and schedule allow.

items.title: OPTION B — Mariposa Grove (alternative to Glacier Point)
items.notes: Only if Glacier Point Road is closed: Grizzly Giant and the California Tunnel Tree. Shuttle from the Welcome Plaza near the South Entrance.

items.title: Glacier Point Rd → Wawona → south exit (CA-41)
items.notes: Exit via the South Entrance toward Oakhurst.

items.title: Lunch in Oakhurst
items.notes: The last proper town before the agricultural valley.

items.title: Oakhurst → Fresno → Three Rivers
items.notes: CA-41 S → CA-180/CA-99 → CA-198 E. A transfer leg, not particularly scenic.

items.title: Relax along the Kaweah River
items.notes: Three Rivers is a small riverside town at the foot of Sequoia: stroll and decompress.

items.title: Night 6 — Three Rivers or Visalia
items.notes: Three Rivers = close to the Sequoia entrance (recommended). Visalia = more convenient/cheaper, but +40 min of driving tomorrow morning.

items.title: Kings Canyon: cut (for good reason)
items.notes: With 7 days, a full Kings Canyon visit is the first practical cut: not because it isn't worth it, but because it's logistically inefficient. At most, Grant Grove — if you leave very early and accept a long drive home.

items.title: Three Rivers → Giant Forest
items.notes: Generals Highway: steep, full of switchbacks, ~5,000 ft of climbing. Not recommended for long vehicles. Gas up before heading up (there's none in the park).

items.title: General Sherman Tree
items.notes: The largest tree on Earth (by volume). Early = easy parking and few people.

items.title: Congress Trail (at least part of it)
items.notes: A 2-mile loop among the giants: the House and Senate Groups, the President Tree. Even half an hour is worth it.

items.title: Moro Rock
items.notes: ~350 steps carved into the granite, with jaw-dropping views of the Great Western Divide. Not to be missed if the sky is clear.

items.title: Tunnel Log
items.notes: The sequoia that fell in 1937 — and you drive right through it. Obligatory photo.

items.title: Crescent Meadow (if you have time)
items.notes: The 'Gem of the Sierra' according to John Muir. Bears sometimes graze here — keep your distance.

items.title: Quick lunch / picnic in the park
Sequoia → Bakersfield
items.notes: Descend the Generals Highway (use engine braking!), then CA-65 S.

items.title: Bakersfield → Pasadena
items.notes: CA-99 → I-5 S over the Grapevine → CA-134. Expect possible traffic heading back into LA.

items.title: Arrival in Pasadena
items.notes: End of the loop: ~1,500 miles of coast, cities and parks. Celebration dinner!

checklist.text: Book night 5 near Yosemite (Valley > El Portal > Mariposa) — THE MOST IMPORTANT
Book hotel in Three Rivers / Visalia (night 6)
Book hotel in San Francisco (nights 3-4) — check parking costs
Check Highway 1 / Big Sur on Caltrans a few days before
Buy the 'America the Beautiful' pass ($80, worth it with 2+ parks) or single entries
Check that Glacier Point Road is open
(Only if it's a priority) Book the first morning tour at Hearst Castle
Download offline maps (Google Maps): Yosemite and Sequoia have no signal
Cash for the 17-Mile Drive toll (~$12) and state park parking

suggestions.title: El Matador State Beach (Malibu)
suggestions.notes: Postcard-perfect beach with sea stacks and rock arches. Requires the scenic detour via PCH instead of US-101: beautiful, but slower.

suggestions.title: Santa Barbara Courthouse — observation tower
suggestions.notes: Free climb up the clock tower: 360° views over red-tile roofs, ocean and mountains. Steps away from State Street.

suggestions.title: OstrichLand USA (Buellton)
suggestions.notes: Feed ostriches and emus just two minutes from Solvang. A gloriously absurd, fun stop — perfect for a break.

suggestions.title: Madonna Inn (San Luis Obispo)
suggestions.notes: America's kitschiest hotel: shocking-pink dining room, giant desserts. Stop just for a coffee and the famous Pink Champagne Cake.

suggestions.title: Montaña de Oro State Park
suggestions.notes: Wild, crowd-free cliffs near Morro Bay. The Bluff Trail (2 miles, flat) is one of California's finest coastal walks.

suggestions.title: Moonstone Beach (Cambria)
suggestions.notes: Wooden boardwalk above a beach of polished pebbles. A relaxing stop between Morro Bay and Hearst Castle.

suggestions.title: Nepenthe (Big Sur)
suggestions.notes: Legendary terrace 800 feet above the ocean, beloved by writers and artists since 1949. Even just a coffee at Café Kevah makes the stop worthwhile.

suggestions.title: Pfeiffer Beach — purple sand
suggestions.notes: A beach with purple-tinged sand and the Keyhole Arch (spectacular at sunset). Narrow, unsigned access road; parking $12.

suggestions.title: Point Sur Lighthouse
suggestions.notes: An 1889 lighthouse on a volcanic rock rising out of the sea. Dramatic view from the Highway 1 turnout; interior tours run on fixed schedules.

suggestions.title: Monterey Bay Aquarium
suggestions.notes: One of the world's best aquariums: sea otters, giant tuna, hypnotic jellyfish. Fair warning: those 2.5 hours come out of Santa Cruz / Half Moon Bay.

suggestions.title: Old Fisherman’s Wharf (Monterey)
suggestions.notes: Historic wharf with free clam chowder samples and sea lions lounging under the walkways. A great pick for dinner on night 2.

suggestions.title: The Mystery Spot (Santa Cruz)
suggestions.notes: A cabin in the woods where gravity seems to go haywire: impossible slopes and optical illusions. Very touristy but fun; book online.

suggestions.title: Lombard Street (San Francisco)
suggestions.notes: The world's most crooked street: eight flower-lined hairpin turns on Russian Hill. Steps from the Powell-Hyde cable car terminus.

suggestions.title: Twin Peaks (San Francisco)
suggestions.notes: The city's highest viewpoint: skyline, bay and Golden Gate all at once. Magical at sunset — bring a hoodie (it's windy!).

suggestions.title: Alcatraz
suggestions.notes: The world's most famous prison, with an excellent audio tour. HEADS UP: tickets must be booked weeks in advance, and the visit takes half a day.

suggestions.title: Muir Woods National Monument
suggestions.notes: Giant coast redwoods 30 minutes from the Golden Gate. Gorgeous, but parking is by ADVANCE RESERVATION ONLY and it eats into city time.

suggestions.title: Valley View (Yosemite)
suggestions.notes: El Capitan and Bridalveil reflected in the Merced River: the photo spot almost everyone misses. A small turnout on Northside Drive on the way out of the Valley.

suggestions.title: Mirror Lake (Yosemite)
suggestions.notes: Easy walk (2 miles round trip) to the little lake that mirrors Half Dome. It can be nearly dry by late summer: at its best in spring/early summer.

suggestions.title: Vernal Fall Footbridge (Mist Trail)
suggestions.notes: The first stretch of Yosemite's best-loved trail: 1.6 miles round trip with a view of the waterfall from the footbridge. At full flow you will get wet — that's the fun of it.

suggestions.title: General Grant Tree (Kings Canyon)
suggestions.notes: The world's second-largest tree, the “Nation's Christmas Tree”. The only sensible taste of Kings Canyon on a 7-day trip: a ~1-hour detour from the route.
